A two-stream graph attention network (GAT) is designed for the task of room segmentation.
The primal stream takes as input the primal graph that encodes line endpoints as vertices and line segments as edges.
Furthermore, the two streams should enhance each other rather than being separated.

Floorplan understanding is crucial for various indoor applications such as interior designs, room space modeling, and real estates.
Traditional methods treat a floorplan as an image and rely on low-level image processing and predefined rules to detect walls, doors, and windows.
Deep learning-based floorplan segmentation dominates the field and achieves superior performance.
Previous works employ a fully convolutional network (FCN) to recognize room types, boundaries, and indoor furniture.
Zeng et al. design a deep multi-task network to learn room types and boundaries, respectively, and model their spatial interactions by boundary-guided attentions.
Latest works employ more powerful semantic segmentation networks and utilize object detection to detect furniture and texts, enhancing segmentation performance.

edge: The novel graph attention module is designed to modulate the attention operations using information from two different streams, enhancing the tasks of floorplan segmentation.
The goal is to perform semantic segmentation on 2D vector roughcast floorplans, dividing them into different room regions.
The input consists of geometric line segments representing wall structures, which are extended and divided into polygonal regions for further processing.
Polygonal regions are calculated with their feature embeddings, as described in Sec. 3.1 and Sec. 3.2.
Two-stream GNN is used for segmentation, where polygonal regions are classified into different room types.
Primal stream of GNN predicts whether a line segment separates different room regions.
Dual stream of GNN classifies partitioned polygon regions into different room types.
Aesthetically pleasing room layouts contain rooms separated into private areas like bedrooms.
Rational room layouts contain rooms connected without wall separation, like living rooms, corridors, dining rooms, etc.
Interior designers partition room space with extended wall structures to create regular and rectangular room regions.
Line segments are extended to divide the design space into a set of polygonal regions.
Semantic segmentation is achieved by classifying the room type of each partitioned region.

After constructing two graphs, we embed vertex and edge attributes as input features.
Despite the equivalence of raster images and vector floorplans, features from raster images help improve spatial receptive field.
The features obtained from the raster image are used along with geometric features for vertex embedding.
The embedding for primal graph vertices V is given by v ∈ Vfv = xv + pev, where xv ∈ RC denotes the feature vector indexed by the coordinates of v from the rasterized floorplan image feature.
The embedding for primal graph vertices V includes the sinusoidal positional encoding of vertex positions.
The embedding of a dual vertex v∗ captures the features of a polygonal region by computing it as the average feature of points sampled from the polygonal region enclosed by Gv∗ = {Vv∗ ,Ev∗}.
Interior points Iv∗ are sampled by using the centers of Delaunay triangles within the polygonal region enclosed by Gv∗.
The primal edges are embedded by mapping their attributes to input features, as primal edges contain attributes other than endpoint positions.

The output features of the two-stream GNN are used for prediction tasks.
The edge features in G produced by the primal stream are fed into a binary classification head to predict whether or not an edge in G separates different room regions.
The vertex features in G∗ from the dual stream are projected into the probabilities of the vertices belonging to different room types.
We optimize the two-stream network by minimizing the boundary classification loss for lines Lp and room type classification loss for regions Lr.
Lp classifies primal line segments as lying on the boundaries of rooms by the binary cross entropy. Lr classifies dual polygonal regions into corresponding room types by the cross entropy.
The overall loss L is defined as L = λp · Lp + Lr, where the hyper-parameter λp is set to 0.5 empirically.

RI is defined as the product of the average IoU of matched rooms and F1-score.
